**St Benet's Abbey**, also known as **St Benet's at Holme** or **St Benet Hulme**, was a medieval [monastery](/wiki/Monastery "Monastery") of the [Order of Saint Benedict](/wiki/Order_of_Saint_Benedict "Order of Saint Benedict") situated at Cow Holm in [Horning](/wiki/Horning "Horning"), [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk "Norfolk"), [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"). It lay on the [River Bure](/wiki/River_Bure "River Bure") within [the Broads](/wiki/The_Broads "The Broads").[[1]](#cite_note-1) St Benet is a medieval English version of the name of St [Benedict of Nursia](/wiki/Benedict_of_Nursia "Benedict of Nursia"), hailed as the founder of western [monasticism](/wiki/Monasticism "Monasticism").
At the period of the [Dissolution of the Monasteries](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Monasteries "Dissolution of the Monasteries") the [abbey](/wiki/Abbey "Abbey")'s possessions were in effect seized by the crown and assigned to the diocese of Norwich. Though the monastery was supposed to continue as a community, within a few years at least the monks had dispersed. Today there remain only [ruins](/wiki/Ruins "Ruins").

## The Abbey in Anglo-Saxon times

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Benet%27s_Abbey&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: The Abbey in Anglo-Saxon times")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/St_Benet%27s_Abbey%2C_Norfolk.jpg/500px-St_Benet%27s_Abbey%2C_Norfolk.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Benet%27s_Abbey,_Norfolk.jpg)

Gatehouse of St Benet's Abbey in the early morning mist, October 2004. The conical structure is the remains of the later windmill built inside it

The early history of the monastery has to be told tentatively since it is difficult to reconcile the surviving sources with what is known of the bigger picture of the development of the area. It is said that St Benet's was founded on the site of a 9th-century [monastery](/wiki/Monastery "Monastery") where the [hermit](/wiki/Hermit "Hermit") Suneman was martyred by the [Danes](/wiki/Danes_(Germanic_tribe) "Danes (Germanic tribe)"). About the end of the 10th century it was rebuilt by one [Wulfric](/wiki/Wulfric_of_Holme "Wulfric of Holme"). A generation later, c. 1022, [King Canute](/wiki/Cnut_the_Great "Cnut the Great") conferred on it his manors of [Horning](/wiki/Horning "Horning"), [Ludham](/wiki/Ludham "Ludham") and [Neatishead](/wiki/Neatishead "Neatishead").[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Canute appears to have endowed at the same time [another Benedictine monastery](/wiki/Beodricesworth_monastery "Beodricesworth monastery") that was later [Bury St Edmunds Abbey](/wiki/Bury_St_Edmunds_Abbey "Bury St Edmunds Abbey"), at [Beodricsworth](/wiki/Beodricsworth "Beodricsworth"), afterwards known as St Edmundsbury, where since the early 10th century, the relics of the martyred king, [St Edmund](/wiki/Edmund_the_Martyr "Edmund the Martyr") had been venerated. With this new endowment, under the auspices of the [Bishop of Elmham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Norwich "Bishop of Norwich"), the original community was reinforced or replaced by a party consisting of half of the monks of St Benet's Abbey under Prior Uvius or Ufi. They arrived bearing half of all the furniture, books, sacred vestments and other worship items belonging to St Benet's.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Ufi became Bury's first [abbot](/wiki/Abbot "Abbot") and governed until his death in 1043. He was blessed as abbot by the [Bishop of London](/wiki/Bishop_of_London "Bishop of London"). His successor (1044–1065) was [Leofstan](/wiki/Leofstan "Leofstan"), another of the former St Benet's monks.

Other early benefactors of St Benet's included Earl Ralf II of East Anglia and [Edith Swannesha](/wiki/Edith_Swannesha "Edith Swannesha"), concubine to [Harold II](/wiki/Harold_Godwinson "Harold Godwinson").

In 1065 St Benet's Abbey was in good enough condition to establish a cell, later [Rumburgh Priory](/wiki/Rumburgh_Priory "Rumburgh Priory") in [Suffolk](/wiki/Suffolk "Suffolk").[[6]](#cite_note-pagew-6) However, towards the end of the 12th century this became a dependency of [St Mary's Abbey](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Abbey,_York "St Mary's Abbey, York") in [York](/wiki/York "York"). This happened by means of a gift made by the patron, who was either [Stephen of Penthièvre, Count of Tréguier, Lord of Richmond](/wiki/Stephen,_Count_of_Tr%C3%A9guier "Stephen, Count of Tréguier") or his son [Alan of Penthièvre, Earl of Richmond and Cornwall](/wiki/Alan,_1st_Earl_of_Richmond "Alan, 1st Earl of Richmond").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

It was the first abbot of St Benet's Abbey, Elsinus, who procured stone to replace the wooden structure of the abbey's church. This must have been a notable operation since suitable stone does not occur in the vicinity. The work was completed by the second abbot, Thurstan, who when he died in 1064 is reported to have been buried before the altar in the chapel of St Michael within the abbey church.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/St_Bennets_Abbey_Gate.jpg/500px-St_Bennets_Abbey_Gate.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Bennets_Abbey_Gate.jpg)

Carving detail around gate

At the time of the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest") of England, King Harold Godwinson put the [abbot](/wiki/Abbot "Abbot") of St Benet's, in charge of defending the [East Anglian](/wiki/East_Anglia "East Anglia") coast against invasion. The involvement with military naval matters was naturally incumbent upon the abbot, as upon bishops and monastic superiors throughout England, in so far as he was a prominent public personage and landowner in the area and hence an integral element in the feudal system.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) The abbey was responsible for organizing a [shipsoke](/w/index.php?title=Shipsoke&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shipsoke (page does not exist)")[[14]](#cite_note-14) or grouping of several hundreds which then had the obligation of providing a fighting ship. It is possible that the abbey did in fact provide the King Harold's ship.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## The Abbey after the Conquest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Benet%27s_Abbey&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: The Abbey after the Conquest")]

After the Conquest, [William the Conqueror](/wiki/William_the_Conqueror "William the Conqueror") pursued those seen as having supported the defeated Harold and Abbot Aelfwold was outlawed and exiled for a time to Denmark,[[16]](#cite_note-16) and the [abbey](/wiki/Abbey "Abbey")'s estates suffered encroachments by neighbouring landowners and a general campaign of systematic harassment by the tenants of the upcoming Norman magnate Sir Roger Bigod,[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) whom the [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book") gives as holding 187 lordships in [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk "Norfolk") and another 117 in [Suffolk](/wiki/Suffolk "Suffolk"). The harassment was to continue for a long period of time.[[19]](#cite_note-19) In the reign of [Henry II](/wiki/Henry_II_of_England "Henry II of England") (1154–1189) the church at [Ranworth](/wiki/Ranworth "Ranworth"), which was the property of the abbey, was stolen bodily, and being a timber building, was dismantled and spirited away. It took a command from the King to have it returned.[[20]](#cite_note-Snelling_and_Edwards_1983_p4-20)

John of Oxnead (de Oxenedes), a 13th-century monk of St Benet's, says in his [*Chronicle*](/wiki/Chronica_Johannis_de_Oxenedes "Chronica Johannis de Oxenedes") that Abbot Aelfwold was later able to return and resume his post, dying at the abbey as abbot on 14 November 1089.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) He was succeeded as abbot by Ralph, and Ralph in 1101 by Richard, who is credited with having completed the church's western tower and with having hung two large bells there.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Seal_of_St._Benet%27s_Abbey_in_1534.jpg/250px-Seal_of_St._Benet%27s_Abbey_in_1534.jpg)](/wiki/File:Seal_of_St._Benet%27s_Abbey_in_1534.jpg)

Seal of St Benet's Abbey in 1534, as appended to the acceptance of the Act of Supremacy

The site was not immune to natural disasters and in the 13th and 14th centuries there were incidents where violent storms on the coast forced the sea to break through the dunes, causing damage to the abbey. In 1287 to save the horses, they had to be brought from the stables to shelter on higher land in the nave of the church.[[23]](#cite_note-Snelling_and_Edwards_1983_p5-23) The abbey also remained vulnerable to hostile incursions by water and in 1327 by royal licence the site was enclosed by a wall with [battlements](/wiki/Battlements "Battlements"), isolated traces of which still survive.[[23]](#cite_note-Snelling_and_Edwards_1983_p5-23)

Surviving records from the 12th century show that at least some of the abbey's tenants paid their rents in kind or by means of service rendered. At [Swanton Abbott](/wiki/Swanton_Abbott "Swanton Abbott") the lease for a mill and a piece of land were four fat cocks a year, land at [Potter Heigham](/wiki/Potter_Heigham "Potter Heigham") was paid each year with a supply of beer for the monks, another stretch of land at [Banningham](/wiki/Banningham "Banningham") was rented for eight measures of honey and a property in London for a pound of pepper and a pound of cummin, while two churches, one at [Stalham](/wiki/Stalham "Stalham") and one in Norwich had to present the abbey annually with a pound of incense each.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Abbots with connections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Benet%27s_Abbey&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Abbots with connections")]

That the abbey continued to have connections to the court may be shown by the fact that the man who became abbot in 1126 was Conrad, who has been identified with the monk who till then had been prior of [Christ Church Cathedral Priory](/wiki/Christ_Church_Cathedral_Priory "Christ Church Cathedral Priory"), [Canterbury](/wiki/Canterbury "Canterbury"), and who had been confessor to [King Henry I](/wiki/Henry_I_of_England "Henry I of England").[[25]](#cite_note-25) Conrad is said to have brought with him two chasubles and a book that had been the property of St [Dunstan](/wiki/Dunstan "Dunstan"), [Archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury"), together with a chalice Dunstan himself had made. These objects were conserved at St Benet's as [relics](/wiki/Relic "Relic").[[20]](#cite_note-Snelling_and_Edwards_1983_p4-20)

Later that same century another abbot with even stronger connections to power arrived to govern St Benet's. [Hugh](/wiki/Hugh,_abbot_of_Lagny "Hugh, abbot of Lagny") was an illegimitate half-brother to [Cardinal](/wiki/Cardinal_Priest "Cardinal Priest") [William of the White Hands](/wiki/William_of_the_White_Hands "William of the White Hands") and to [Count Henry I of Champagne](/wiki/Henry_I,_Count_of_Champagne "Henry I, Count of Champagne") who was married to [Marie](/wiki/Marie_of_France,_Countess_of_Champagne "Marie of France, Countess of Champagne"), elder daughter of King [Louis VII of France](/wiki/Louis_VII_of_France "Louis VII of France"). Hugh's half-sister was [Adela, Queen of France](/wiki/Adela_of_Champagne "Adela of Champagne") and he was a nephew of [King Stephen of England](/wiki/Stephen,_King_of_England "Stephen, King of England") and of the King's brother [Henry of Blois](/wiki/Henry_of_Blois "Henry of Blois") Henry of Blois, [Bishop of Winchester](/wiki/Bishop_of_Winchester "Bishop of Winchester").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Hugh became a knight and was wounded in battle about 1136. Cared for at [Tiron Abbey](/wiki/Tironensian_Order "Tironensian Order") in [France](/wiki/France "France"), when he recovered he decided to become a monk there. Later he was made abbot of St Benet's (1146-1150) thanks to his uncle King Stephen (or Henry of Blois),[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) the appointment receiving papal confirmation in 1147. To secure this post for Hugh, the previous abbot, Daniel, was deposed.

The story in John of Oxnead's *Chronicle*[[29]](#cite_note-29) is that he was a capable and serious abbot but made powerful enemies who framed him by having a woman slipped into his bed and then sent armed men to punish the supposed crime by castrating him. After the violence, John of Oxnead says, Hugh's uncle King Stephen obtained for him the post of abbot of [Chertsey Abbey](/wiki/Chertsey_Abbey "Chertsey Abbey") (1149-1163) in [Surrey](/wiki/Surrey "Surrey") and at St Benet's Abbot Daniel returned to his post. Subsequently, the death of King Stephen lead to a hostile climate in England for the family and Henry of Blois fled from England in 1155. Hugh followed, returning to Champagne and becoming once more a monk of [Tiron Abbey](/wiki/Tironensian_Order "Tironensian Order"). However, when trouble arose at [Lagny Abbey](/wiki/Lagny_Abbey "Lagny Abbey"), he was made abbot there (1163-1171). Though an active abbot, for some reason was deposed in 1171 and died shortly afterwards, being buried at the abbey.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

## Local cults

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Benet%27s_Abbey&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Local cults")]

Despite elements of success, both material and spiritual, the abbey may have struggled to compete in religious prestige, lacking as it did the relics of an important saint. It seems that alongside a circumscribed veneration for the memory of the hermit Wulfric or Wulfey, who was said to have occupied the site before the abbey's foundation, in the 12-13th centuries the abbey tried to promote the cult of St Margaret of Holm, supposedly a girl killed in the woods at Hoveton St John on 22 May 1170, but this made little or no progress.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)

## Benefactions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Benet%27s_Abbey&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Benefactions")]

The abbey's life had substantially begun with benefactions and despite attempts both surreptitious and aggressive to snatch them away, the benefactions continued. It has been suggested that participation in 1075 of an early patron, [Ralph Guader](/wiki/Ralph_Guader "Ralph Guader"), Earl of East Anglia, in the failed [Revolt of the Earls](/wiki/Revolt_of_the_Earls "Revolt of the Earls") against [William the Conqueror](/wiki/William_the_Conqueror "William the Conqueror") and his subsequent flight to [Brittany](/wiki/Brittany "Brittany") may have caused later patrons to divert their benefactions to other monasteries. Nevertheless, benefactions there were, to the points that by the late 13th century St Benet's had property in 76 parishes.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

One of the abbey's great benefactors was [Sir John Fastolf](/wiki/Sir_John_Fastolf "Sir John Fastolf"), the inspiration for Shakespeare's [Falstaff](/wiki/Falstaff "Falstaff"), who died at [Caister](/wiki/Caister-on-Sea "Caister-on-Sea") and was buried at St Benet's in December 1459, next to his wife Millicent in a new aisle built by Fastolf himself on the south side of the abbey church. The bulk of his fortune passed to [Magdalen College](/wiki/Magdalen_College,_Oxford "Magdalen College, Oxford"), [Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford"), but his intention to establish a [chantry](/wiki/Chantry "Chantry") at [Caister Castle](/wiki/Caister_Castle "Caister Castle") did not materialize.

## Later ownership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Benet%27s_Abbey&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Later ownership")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/StBenetOfHolmAbbey.jpg/250px-StBenetOfHolmAbbey.jpg)](/wiki/File:StBenetOfHolmAbbey.jpg)

Remains of the abbey church nave, looking toward the high altar

After the Dissolution the greater part of the buildings at the site were demolished, with the exclusion of the gatehouse,[[36]](#cite_note-36) which is now a Grade I listed building.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

In the second half of the 18th century, a farmer built a windmill, later converted to a [windpump](/wiki/Windpump "Windpump"), inside the abbey gatehouse, removing the second floor of the gatehouse in the process.

From the early 18th century, active attention was paid to drainage of the marshland around the site. From various surviving illustrations, it appears that a first windmill-powered land drainage was erected around the middle of the 18th century, and some decades later was replaced by another, attached to the front of the ruined gatehouse. By at least 1813, to facilitate movement of the windmill sails the upper floor of the gatehouse was removed to provide room for the sails to turn. The sails survived until at least 1854 but had been destroyed by 1863.

The wind-powered mechanism, which at time ceased operating and is itself now a ruin, is a grade II\* listed building.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Between 1782 and 1886 just along the river from the gatehouse there was a wherryman's riverside pub called *The Chequers*.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

In 1925 the site became one of Britain's first [scheduled ancient monuments](/wiki/Scheduled_monument "Scheduled monument").

In 1993 the main part of the site was bought by the Crown Estate and in 2002 sold to the Norfolk Archaeological Trust which in 2004 purchased the gatehouse and mill from the Diocese of Norwich. The ruins of the church remain the property of the diocese, which has leased them to the Trust for 199 years. In recent years essential conservation repairs have been carried out on the ruins and visits to the site have been facilitated by the laying out of a new car park and access paths, while large numbers of volunteers undertook graffiti recording, molehill and wildlife surveys, and maintenance and provided guides. Aside from several scientific studies, the site has become the focus of intense local interest.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/BenetsCross.jpg/250px-BenetsCross.jpg)](/wiki/File:BenetsCross.jpg)

The modern cross at the high altar of the St Benet's Abbey ruins

On 2 August 1987 a [cross](/wiki/Cross "Cross") made from [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak") from the royal estate at [Sandringham](/wiki/Sandringham_House "Sandringham House") was erected on the high altar.

## Post-Reformation Abbot, Prior and Lay Monks of St Benet-at-Holme

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Benet%27s_Abbey&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Post-Reformation Abbot, Prior and Lay Monks of St Benet-at-Holme")]

See also: [Bishop of Norwich § Bishops during the Reformation](/wiki/Bishop_of_Norwich#Bishops_during_the_Reformation "Bishop of Norwich"), and [Bishop of Norwich § Post-Reformation bishops](/wiki/Bishop_of_Norwich#Post-Reformation_bishops "Bishop of Norwich")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Bishop_at_St_Benets_2017-05.jpg/330px-Bishop_at_St_Benets_2017-05.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bishop_at_St_Benets_2017-05.jpg)

The [Bishop of Norwich](/wiki/Bishop_of_Norwich "Bishop of Norwich") arrives by [wherry](/wiki/Wherry "Wherry") at St Benet's for the 2017 annual service

During the [Dissolution of the Monasteries](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Monasteries "Dissolution of the Monasteries") the [Vicar General](/wiki/Vicar_General "Vicar General") was said to have noted the poverty in which the brothers of St Benet-at-Home committed to live in by contrast to the opulence of the Bishop of Norwich. It is unclear whether this was a deliberate choice to uphold principles of monastic [asceticism](/wiki/Asceticism#Christianity "Asceticism") or due to the high levels of debt the monastery was said to be in. Holding the abbey up as an example of [protestant monasticism](/wiki/Christian_monasticism "Christian monasticism") during the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation"), [Thomas Cromwell](/wiki/Thomas_Cromwell "Thomas Cromwell") persuaded [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII "Henry VIII") to spare the abbey, and unite it into the newly founded [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), and to elevate the Abbot of St-Benet's to the Bishops [cathedra](/wiki/Cathedra "Cathedra") uniting the two roles. It thus became the sole abbey spared during the dissolution, and was protected in statute by an extraordinary act of parliament in 1536. The act stipulated that the Abbot be permitted to forever maintain a Prior and twelve monks at the abbey. This makes it arguably the oldest [Anglican Order of St Benedict](/wiki/Order_of_St_Benedict_(Anglican) "Order of St Benedict (Anglican)") in England. By 1545 however the abbey had collapsed and the brothers had to abandon the site. The lands were leased to a succession of local farmers to pay down the debts. As the Bishops were enrobed amongst the [Lords Spiritual](/wiki/Lords_Spiritual "Lords Spiritual"), the power that prior to the dissolution came with being an abbot was eroded, and successive Bishops of Norwich saw little point in using the title. However since 1939 Bishops of Norwich have once again used the title of Abbot of St Benet's reaffirming their historic role. The Bishop in their role as Abbot appoints a local vicar as Prior of St Benet-at-Holme, who in turn appoints 12 lay monks to assist with his role. Every year on the first Sunday of August the Bishop of Norwich, in their role as Abbot, arrives at St Benet's standing in the bow of a [wherry](/wiki/Norfolk_wherry "Norfolk wherry"), to meet pilgrims, and preach an annual service amongst the ruins of the abbey. The service takes place at the altar beneath the cross. The service follows an Anglican interpretation of the [rule of Saint Benedict](/wiki/Rule_of_Saint_Benedict "Rule of Saint Benedict").[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-:0-44)[[45]](#cite_note-bruce-45)
